title: Why does a vendor's request to attribute consideration between taxable and exempt supplies under section 10(22) not qualify as a VAT ruling or decision?
text: The request does not qualify as an application for either a VAT ruling nor a decision because there is no discretion afforded to the Commissioner as to how to determine the different parts of the consideration as contemplated in section 10(22).

title: When did the changes to the wording of section 72 apply to new applications?
text: The changes to the wording of section 72 only apply to new applications made on or after 21 July 2019.

title: What is an example of a situation where a recipient may be permitted to issue tax invoices instead of the supplier?
text: An example is where a farmer (the supplier) takes produce to a co-operative which will only be sold at a later stage, once the quality and quantity of the produce has been determined. Since the price depends on factors outside the farmer's control, the farmer is not in a position to issue an invoice when the produce is delivered for sale. In such cases, SARS may permit the co-operative (recipient) to issue the tax invoices instead of the supplier.

title: Under the invoice basis method, must vendors account for VAT on supplies even if payment has not been received?
text: Yes, vendors must account for the full amount of output tax on any supplies made in the tax period, even where payment has not yet been received from the recipient. Similarly, the full amount of input tax may be deducted on supplies received in the tax period, even where payment has not yet been made.

title: What is the output tax that the paper manufacturer must declare for selling 2 rolls of uncoated print paper sheets for R92,00?
text: The paper manufacturer must declare output tax of R12,00 (R92,00 × 15 / 115) for the 2 rolls of paper sold.

title: What is the threshold for the decrease in taxable use of capital goods that triggers an adjustment requirement?
text: The decrease in the extent of the taxable use of the capital goods or services must exceed 10% to trigger an adjustment requirement.

title: What happens to the requirement for the VAT number of the recipient to be reflected on a tax invoice when a person's VAT registration is backdated?
text: When a person's VAT registration is backdated and certain supplies were received before the registration date, the requirement for the VAT number of the recipient to be reflected on the tax invoice will not apply. As a result, tax invoices received for supplies made to that vendor before the date of registration may still be considered as valid proof for input tax documentary requirements.

title: What is included in a notice of assessment issued by SARS to a taxpayer?
text: A notice of assessment includes the applicable tax liability or tax refund, along with all of the details prescribed under section 96 of the TA Act.

title: How is notional input tax calculated on second-hand goods?
text: The notional input tax is calculated by multiplying the tax fraction by the lesser of the consideration paid or the OMV. Where the OMV is less than the consideration paid, the OMV will be used to calculate the notional input tax deduction.

title: What is Public Notice 787 and what does it regulate?
text: Public Notice 787: Electronic form of record keeping under section 30(1)(b) of the Tax Administration Act, 2011 regulates how records for tax purposes are to be retained in electronic form. It was published on 1 October 2012 in GG 35733 with immediate effect.

title: What is the refund method for amounts between R300 and R3,000?
text: For refunds between R300 and R3,000, the VRA will issue the qualifying purchaser with a pre-paid debit card which can be used worldwide.

title: Can you deduct VAT on goods acquired for private use if you are registered for VAT?
text: No. If you are registered for VAT because you make both taxable and non-taxable supplies, you may not deduct any VAT charged on goods or services acquired for private use or other non-taxable purposes.

title: On what types of supplies can a registered person charge VAT?
text: A registered person can only charge VAT on taxable supplies. They may not charge VAT on supplies which are exempt from VAT or supplies which fall outside the scope of VAT (supplies which are not in the course or furtherance of an 'enterprise').

title: What amendment was made to the definition of 'enterprise' regarding cross-border leases of foreign-owned parts?
text: The scope of proviso (xiii) to the definition of 'enterprise' was extended to include the leasing of foreign-owned parts relating to foreign-owned ships, aircraft or rolling stock if those parts are leased under a separate agreement. This clarifies that a non-resident lessor is not regarded as carrying on an enterprise in the Republic when such leasing occurs, subject to certain conditions being met.

title: What output tax must the supermarket declare per box of paper sold to its customers?
text: The supermarket must declare output tax of R1,50 on each box of paper sold to its customers at R11,50 each (inclusive of R1,50 VAT).

title: What happens if a diesel refund is found to be incorrectly deducted?
text: Any diesel refund which is found to be incorrectly deducted and paid would have to be paid back to SARS, together with any interest and any other amount that may be levied as a result of that non-compliance.

title: How is the value component (A) in the adjustment formula determined?
text: Component A represents the lesser of either the adjusted cost of the capital goods or the OMV (Open Market Value) of the capital goods.

title: What are the three approved categories of fixed cut-off dates under the 10-day rule?
text: The three approved categories of fixed cut-off dates subject to the 10-day rule are: a specific day of the week, a specific date of a calendar month, and a fixed day determined in accordance and consistent with the vendor's commercial accounting periods.

title: What happens if an agent does not have the required documentation for gold supply?
text: If the agent is not in possession of the required documentation, the agent will be liable to account for output tax at the standard rate on the supply of the gold.

title: Where must the increase or decrease in the value of the supply of valuable metal as a result of a debit- or credit note event be reported?
text: The increase or decrease in the value of the supply of valuable metal as a result of a debit- or credit note event must be reported in Field 3 of the VAT201 return, with the normal time of supply rules under the VAT Act applying.

title: Who is considered a vendor for VAT purposes?
text: A vendor includes any person who is registered or is required to be registered for VAT. However, where the Commissioner has determined the date from which a person is a vendor, a person shall be a vendor from that date.

title: What is the limitation on deductions if the deduction was not previously permitted in accordance with a practice generally prevailing?
text: If the deduction was not previously permitted in accordance with a practice generally prevailing, the deduction is limited to six months before the tax period in which the deduction is made.

title: What types of proof can be provided to demonstrate that payment has already exceeded or will exceed R50 000 within a 12-month period after the date of application?
text: Proof of expenditure incurred or to be incurred or capital goods acquired in connection with the enterprise and proof of payment or an extended payment agreement can be provided as evidence.

title: What happens if cash relating to a transaction is stolen or misappropriated? Can a vendor issue a credit note and deduct input tax?
text: No. If cash relating to a transaction has been stolen or misappropriated, this does not entitle the vendor to issue a credit note and deduct input tax thereon. The VAT Act does not provide for a deduction or adjustment in such cases.

title: What is the purpose of a sanitised template in the ruling application process?
text: A sanitised template is a template to be completed and submitted by the applicant upon application for a ruling, to assist in streamlining the sanitisation and publication process.

title: What should be done if the apportionment method will not yield a fair and reasonable result after changes?
text: A ruling should be obtained to apply another method which results in a more fair and reasonable proportion of VAT being deducted as input tax in the year that the changes occur, and in any subsequent years.

title: What is excluded from qualifying as the supply of commercial accommodation?
text: A dwelling supplied in terms of an agreement for the letting and hiring thereof is specifically excluded from qualifying as the supply of commercial accommodation.

title: In which fields of the VAT201 return should input tax relating to local capital purchases and imported capital goods be deducted?
text: Fields 14 and 14A of the VAT201 return are where input tax relating to local capital purchases and imported capital goods, respectively, will be deducted.

title: What is the condition regarding input tax deduction for irrecoverable debts when a vendor accounts for tax on the payments basis?
text: A vendor may not make an input tax deduction in respect of a debt which has become irrecoverable if the vendor accounts for tax on the payments basis, except to the extent that the vendor was required to account for output tax on the invoice basis (that is in the case of supplies made for a consideration of R100 000 or more) and a portion thereof has become irrecoverable.

title: What are deemed supplies in the context of VAT?
text: Deemed supplies are transactions where a registered vendor may be required to declare an amount of output tax even though they have not actually supplied any goods or services. The VAT Act contains deeming provisions which widen the range of transactions subject to VAT and clarify instances where certain transactions will be deemed to be taxable or not, or deemed not to be supplied in the course or furtherance of an enterprise.

title: What are the four conditions that must be met for the exclusion from the definition of 'enterprise' to apply?
text: The four conditions are: (1) The lessee (recipient) must be a resident of the Republic; (2) The lessor (supplier) must be a non-resident and must not be a registered vendor in the Republic; (3) The goods must be for use wholly or partly in the Republic; and (4) The contracting parties must agree in writing that the recipient will enter the goods for home consumption and be liable to pay VAT on importation, and will not be reimbursed by the lessor for such VAT.

title: Where can vendors find more details about the domestic reverse charge regulations?
text: More details regarding the domestic reverse charge can be found in Public Notice 2200 as published in GG 46598 dated 24 June 2022.

title: Which VAT scheme applies if all invoices and payments for goods occurred before 2 May 2014, even if the goods are delivered after that date?
text: The rules under the previous VAT Export Incentive Scheme will apply if all invoices and payments in relation to the goods have occurred before 2 May 2014, even if the goods are delivered (exported) after 2 May 2014.

title: What must happen to goods after collection in an indirect export by road or rail arrangement?
text: After collection, the goods must be stored and consolidated by the agent in a warehouse until the consolidated consignment of goods is exported to the qualifying purchaser in the export country, and the goods must be exported within 90 days.

title: What are the three categories of fixed cut-off dates approved by SARS under the 10-day rule?
text: The three categories are: a specific day of the week, a specific date of a calendar month, and a fixed day determined in accordance and consistent with the vendor's commercial accounting periods.

title: What clarification was provided regarding the deemed supply in section 8(8) concerning short-term insurance?
text: The provisos to section 8(8) have been reworded and individually numbered to clarify that the deemed supply in section 8(8) only arises in respect of cash indemnity payments made to, or on behalf of, the insured party under a contract of insurance.

title: Are sales from old plant and machinery included or excluded when calculating taxable supplies for VAT registration?
text: Sales from old plant, machinery or other capital assets when replacing them with new assets should be excluded when calculating taxable supplies for VAT registration.

title: What types of supplies are exempt from taxation according to this list?
text: Exempt supplies include certain financial services, supplies by associations not for gain of donated goods or services, rental of accommodation in dwellings including employee housing, certain educational services, services of employee organisations such as trade unions, certain services to members of sectional title, share block or old age schemes funded out of levies, public road and railway transport of fare-paying passengers and their luggage, and childcare services in a crèche or after-school care centre.

title: Where must the input tax be claimed in a VAT201 return when the DRC applies?
text: The input tax must be claimed in field 18 of the VAT201 return.

title: In Example 5, what was the adjustment amount when S changed from payments basis to invoice basis?
text: The adjustment amount was R35,000. This was calculated by taking debtors of R250,000 and subtracting creditors of R215,000, resulting in a difference of R35,000.

title: What form must be used to lodge an appeal and who can sign it?
text: The appeal must be on the form prescribed by the Commissioner (DISP01 – Notice of Appeal form) and must be signed by you or your authorised representative.

title: What is the Refund Dashboard and who can access it?
text: The Refund Dashboard is a function that enables vendors to view the reasons why their refunds may not have been paid out and what actions may be required of them. Only the status of refunds from March 2011 onwards will be displayed. This function is available on eFiling for registered eFilers. Vendors who call the Service Centre or visit a SARS Service Centre can also request assistance from SARS agents who have access to the dashboard.

title: How must VAT payments be made according to the text?
text: Payments have to be done electronically or over the counter at the branch of an approved bank. SARS branches or offices no longer accept manual forms of payment of the VAT.

title: What burden of proof applies when a review application has been made in the High Court regarding a jeopardy assessment?
text: When a review application has been made in the High Court in respect of a jeopardy assessment, SARS bears the burden of proving that the jeopardy assessment was reasonable in the circumstances.

title: What figures can vendors use to calculate the estimated percentage for deducting input tax in the current year?
text: Vendors are allowed to calculate the estimated percentage using the turnover figures from the previous year's financial statements, and apply that percentage for deducting input tax in each and every individual tax period for the current year.

title: When do the Export Regulations generally apply?
text: The Export Regulations generally apply to supplies made on or after 2 May 2014.

title: What is a key characteristic of zero-rated supplies in terms of input tax?
text: Vendors making zero-rated supplies are still able to deduct input tax in full on the goods or services acquired in the making of the zero-rated supplies.

title: What information is not required on an abridged tax invoice?
text: On an abridged tax invoice, the name, address and VAT registration number of the recipient and the quantity or volume do not need to be specified, though all other requirements of a full tax invoice must be met.

title: What is the current standard rate of VAT in South Africa?
text: The standard rate of VAT is currently 15%.

title: What is the main distinction between a request for a Commissioner decision and an application for a VAT Ruling?
text: The main distinction is that a VAT Ruling pertains to the interpretation of the VAT Act whereas a decision constitutes the exercising of a discretion by the Commissioner.

title: How should debit notes issued and credit notes received be reflected on the VAT201 return?
text: Debit notes issued and credit notes received must be reflected in Field 12 – Output tax on the VAT201 return.

title: What types of goods are supplied under an Instalment Credit Agreement?
text: The goods supplied in terms of an ICA relate to corporeal movable goods and plant and machinery.

title: What is the status of VAT 404 – Guide for Vendors as an official publication?
text: VAT 404 – Guide for Vendors is not an 'official publication' as defined in section 1 of the TA Act and accordingly does not create a practice generally prevailing under section 5 of that Act.

title: What is the deadline for submitting a VAT refund application to the VRA when goods are exported through a designated commercial port where the VRA is not present?
text: The application, together with all required documentation, must be received by the VRA within 90 days from the date of export.

title: What is the minimum consideration threshold below which a tax invoice does not have to be issued?
text: If the consideration for the supply is less than R50, a tax invoice does not have to be issued.

title: If the 2018 financial statements indicated an apportionment percentage of 80%, what output tax adjustment would be required in field 12 of the VAT201?
When does the effective date of application of a change in an approved methodology take effect?
text: The effective date of application of a change in an approved methodology will be within the year of assessment (where the vendor is a taxpayer) or within 12 months ending on the last day of February or the last day of the vendor's financial year (where the vendor is not a taxpayer), during which the application for the aforementioned method was made.

title: What types of supplies does the municipal rates division make?
text: The municipal rates division makes only taxable supplies.

title: When is a vendor entitled to make an input tax deduction for goods or services held for non-taxable purposes?
text: A vendor is entitled to make an input tax deduction where goods or services are held for exempt, private or other non-taxable purposes and subsequently applied by the vendor for consumption, use or supply in the course of making taxable supplies.

title: What is the definition of a motor car under the VAT Act?
text: A motor car is defined as a vehicle which has three or more wheels, is normally used on public roads, and is constructed or converted mainly or wholly for carrying passengers.

title: Are there conditions that must be met for interest to be paid on delayed refunds?
text: Yes, interest is only paid if certain conditions are met as a refund may be subject to the finalisation of the verification, inspection or audit of the refund.

title: Under what circumstances can a senior SARS official condone a late objection filing?
text: A senior SARS official may condone an objection filed up to 30 business days late if satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for the delay. If an objection is filed more than 30 business days late, a senior SARS official must be satisfied that exceptional circumstances exist. However, if the basis of the objection is a change to a practice generally prevailing, no condonation is possible as there are no grounds on which SARS can condone late filing in such cases.

title: What is the VAT amount charged on the transaction?
What is the treatment of customs duties and upliftment for goods imported from BLNS countries?
text: Goods imported from BLNS countries are not subject to customs duties and the 10% upliftment in value is not applied if the goods have their origin in a BLNS country. However, VAT is still payable on the importation at the standard rate on the value for customs purposes, unless the goods are specifically exempt from VAT on importation.

title: Under which section of the VAT Act does the Commissioner have a legislative framework to issue VAT rulings?
text: Section 41B of the VAT Act provides the legislative framework for the Commissioner to continue issuing binding VAT class rulings and VAT rulings.

title: What are the main topics covered in Chapter 4 of the VAT 404 Guide for Vendors?
text: Chapter 4 covers accounting basis for VAT, including: introduction, invoice basis, payments basis, change of accounting basis, and special cases such as instalment credit agreements, fixed property, consideration over R100,000, second-hand goods, supplies affected by VAT rate changes, and supplies of valuable metal under the domestic reverse charge.

title: How is the consideration in money for the motor car fringe benefit calculated according to VAT Regulation 2835?
text: Consideration in money = determined value of the motor car × 0.003 (0.3%)

title: What VAT tax fraction must be used to calculate VAT on the adjustment for the computer system sold on 15 May 2018?
text: The tax fraction 15/115 must be used because the time of supply for the adjustment is after the VAT rate increase from 14% to 15% on 1 April 2018.

title: How should input tax be treated when goods or services are acquired partly for making taxable supplies and partly for exempt or other non-taxable purposes?
text: Input tax should be deducted as per the apportionment ratio (the extent of taxable supplies).

title: What happens to a practice generally prevailing when SARS appeals a judgment that materially changes it?
text: If SARS appeals a judgment which materially changes a practice generally prevailing, that practice will remain valid and applicable until the final outcome of the appeal.

title: What information about income streams must be included in an apportionment application?
text: A list of all income streams must be provided including the value thereof, whether such income results from various business activities carried on by the business, and whether those income streams are treated as taxable, exempt or out-of-scope for VAT purposes.

title: What should you do first when debtors and creditors balances include VAT charged at different standard rates?
text: You will first have to identify the separate debtors and creditors balances that include the different standard rates of VAT and then apply the relevant tax fraction to each part.

title: Who bears the burden of proof when a review application has been made in the High Court regarding a jeopardy assessment?
text: SARS bears the burden of proving that the jeopardy assessment was reasonable in the circumstances.

title: When can an abridged tax invoice be issued instead of a full tax invoice?
text: An abridged tax invoice may be issued if the consideration for the supply is less than R5 000 (including tax), except when that supply is a zero-rated supply.

title: Which items are exempt from tax if the employer reimburses the employee for actual expenditure incurred during a residential transfer?
text: The following items are exempt from tax if the employer reimburses the employee for actual expenditure incurred: bond registration and legal fees paid in respect of a new residence that has been purchased; transfer duty paid in respect of the new residence; cancellation fees paid for bond cancellation on previous residence; agent's commission paid on sale of previous residence; new school uniforms; replacement of curtains; motor vehicle registration fees; and telephone, water and electricity connection.

title: Who retains ownership of gains or losses from vesting events?
text: The employee/director retains the vesting event (i.e. a gain or loss) in their hands.

title: From when should tax deductions be made for employees between 65 and 74 years old?
text: Employees' tax deductions for persons between the ages of 65 and 74 years must be made according to tables from the beginning of the tax period (e.g. 1 March) during which the employee turns 65 and not as from the month the qualifying age between the ages of 65 and 74 years is attained.

title: Which source code should be used for lump sum payments prior to 1 October 2007 from a Pension or RAF?
text: Lump sum payments prior to 1 October 2007 from a Pension or RAF should reflect under code 3903.

title: Does a transfer that does not necessitate a change of residence qualify for the transfer costs exemption?
text: No. A transfer that does not necessitate a change of residence does not fall within the ambit of the exemption under Section 10(1)(nB).

title: Under which code should employees' tax deducted for an independent contractor be reflected on the IRP5/IT3(a) certificate?
text: Employees' tax deducted for an independent contractor, whether calculated according to the deduction tables or a tax directive, must be reflected as PAYE under code 3616 on the IRP5/IT3(a) certificate.

title: What documentation must an employer keep on file if they choose not to deduct Employees' Tax for a qualifying employee?
text: The employer must keep a copy of each page of the employee's passport and a copy of the relevant contract for the services to be rendered in a foreign country.

title: Are amounts awarded in respect of unfair dismissals subject to employees' tax deduction?
text: Yes, amounts awarded in respect of unfair dismissals are remuneration as defined in the Fourth Schedule and are therefore subject to deduction of Employees' Tax, as they are received or accrued in respect of the relinquishment, termination, loss, repudiation, cancellation or variation of any office or employment.

title: What is the interest rate for a debt denominated in a currency other than the Republic's currency?
text: The rate of interest will be the equivalent of the country's repurchase rate plus 100 basis points.

title: Where can employers find detailed information about fringe benefits?
text: Employers can refer to the Guide for Employers in respect of Fringe Benefits – External Guide, which is available on the SARS website www.sars.gov.za, for detailed information about determining the amount which must be included in the taxable remuneration of an employee due to fringe benefits being granted.

title: In what circumstances must a capital gain or loss be disregarded for a restricted equity instrument?
text: Any capital gain or loss must be disregarded in respect of any restricted equity instrument where the restricted equity instrument is replaced with another restricted equity instrument, or the taxpayer disposes the restricted equity instrument to any person by a non-arm's length disposal or a disposal to a connected person.

title: How should employers calculate employees' tax on special remuneration paid to proto teams?
text: Employers must calculate the employees' tax deductible from special remuneration in the same manner as tax on a bonus is calculated. The special remuneration paid to proto teams is subject to the rating formula contained in Section 5(10) which can only be applied on assessment.

title: Which document provides guidance on Tax Deduction Tables for weekly pay periods?
text: Document PAYE-GEN-01-G01-A01 provides the Weekly Tax Deduction Tables.

title: What is the total employees' tax on an advance salary of R84 000 received by a monthly paid employee below 65, where the monthly salary is R28 000?
text: The total employees' tax on the advance salary of R84 000 is R12 756 (calculated as R4 252 x 3, where R4 252 is the tax on R28 000 according to the monthly tables).

title: What is the primary tax rebate amount for individuals in 2026?
text: The primary tax rebate for individuals in 2026 is R17 235.

title: What code should be used to reflect the taxable portion of bursaries for grade R to 12 and NQF level 1 to 4?
text: Code 3809 must be used to reflect the taxable portion of bursaries for grade R to 12 and NQF level 1 to 4.

title: What steps are taken to calculate the tax on commission for one month?
text: First, calculate the tax on the combined salary and commission (R21 685) according to monthly tables, which equals R2 624. Then, subtract the tax on the monthly salary alone (R17 185), which is R1 651. This gives the tax on commission for one month: R2 624 - R1 651 = R973.

title: What are the methods through which an application to register as an employer can be made?
text: An application to register as an employer must be made via eFiling or at the SARS Branch.

title: How should an employer request an EMP201 declaration from SARS?
text: An employer must request EMP201 declaration from SARS via eFiling, via e@syFile™ Employer, or at a SARS Branch.

title: What is the total employees' tax deductible for December in the given example?
text: The total employees' tax deductible for December is R4 726, which comprises the tax on the December salary of R4 331 plus the tax on the backdated increased salary of R395.

title: In the production bonus example, what is the Employees' Tax deductible for August?
text: The Employees' Tax deductible for August is R7,988. This consists of the tax on the production bonus of R12,500 paid in August (R3,736) plus the tax on the salary of R28,000 for August according to the monthly table (R4,252).

title: What are the two codes used to reflect taxable and non-taxable portions of an arbitration award on a certificate?
text: The relevant taxable and non-taxable portions of an arbitration award reflect on certificate under Code 3608 (taxable amount) and Code 3602 (non-taxable amount).

title: What is the purpose of an exemption certificate (IRP30) issued to a labour broker?
text: An exemption certificate (IRP30) issued by SARS to a labour broker will absolve employers from having to deduct Employees' tax from any payments made to such labour brokers.

title: What are fringe benefits according to the Seventh Schedule?
text: Fringe benefits are taxable benefits deemed to have been granted by the employer if the employee is granted one of the benefits described in Paragraph 2 of the Seventh Schedule, including: acquisition of an asset at less than actual value, right of use of a motor vehicle, right of use of an asset (excluding motor vehicle and residential accommodation), meals and refreshments, residential accommodation, free or cheap services, low or interest free loans, subsidy in respect of loans, payment of employee's debt or release from debt obligation, medical fund contributions, medical costs, contributions to a benefit fund, contribution to bargaining council, payment to an insurer under an insurance policy, and any benefit granted to the relative of employee and others.

title: What is the stop loss benefit in share incentive schemes and how is it taxed?
text: The stop loss benefit is a benefit that can accrue in terms of share incentive schemes. It is exempt from tax if the employee does not receive or become entitled to receive any compensation or consideration other than the repayment of any portion of the purchase price actually paid by him. Employees' tax must not be deducted from the exempted amount and should be reflected under code 3714.

title: What fringe benefit arises when an employer pays insurance premiums for employees?
text: A simultaneous fringe benefit inclusion arises for employees when an employer pays insurance premiums. The fringe benefit will arise on a monthly basis in respect of employer-owned insurance policies for the direct and indirect benefit of employees.

title: What types of workers can a labour broker provide to a client?
text: Workers provided by a labour broker can be any person, including members and/or employees of a close corporation, directors and/or employees of a company, trustees and/or employees of a trading trust, proprietors and/or employees of a business, or partners and/or employees of a partnership.

title: What is the employer's liability if an employee does not ultimately qualify for the tax exemption after the employer chose not to deduct Employees' Tax?
text: The employer will be held personally liable for any losses that SARS may suffer due to the non-deduction of the full amount of employees' tax.

title: What is the tax rate for a person with a taxable income of R500,000 in the 2025/2026 tax year?
text: For a taxable income of R500,000 (which falls in the R370,501 – R512,800 bracket), the tax is calculated as: R77,362 + 31% of taxable income above R370,500. This equals R77,362 + (31% × R129,500) = R77,362 + R40,145 = R117,507.

title: What must trustees or fund administrators do before a lump sum benefit from a pension fund may be paid?
text: Trustees or fund administrators must apply for a tax directive at SARS before a lump sum benefit from a pension, pension preservation, provident, provident preservation or retirement annuity fund may be paid.

title: What three elements are required by the Fourth Schedule before employees' tax may be deducted?
text: The three elements required before employees' tax may be deducted are: an employer, an employee, and remuneration being paid by the employer to the employee. The employer must determine the employment relationship to classify the worker correctly in order to determine the rate which must be applied to deduct employees' tax.

title: What is the Employees' Tax deductible for October when a monthly paid employee receives a salary of R28,000 and a bonus of R14,800?
text: The Employees' Tax deductible for October is R8,156. This is calculated by determining the tax on the total remuneration of R350,800 (R336,000 annual equivalent salary plus R14,800 bonus) according to annual tables (R54,937), subtracting the tax on the annual equivalent salary of R336,000 (R51,033), which gives tax on the bonus of R3,904, and then adding the tax on the salary of R28,000 according to the monthly table (R4,252).

title: What is the total value of relocation expenses that are subject to employees' tax deduction?
text: The total value of expenses subject to employees' tax deduction is R19,600, comprising the loss on sale of the previous residence (R12,000) and architect's fees for the design of the new residence (R7,600). These items must be reflected under code 3713.

title: In what year can an employer claim expenditure for amounts paid to employees?
text: The employer can claim expenditure in the year that the employer pays that amount to the employee.

title: What is the key difference between an employee contract and an independent contractor contract?
text: The key difference lies in the object of the contract. An employee contract (contract of service) involves placing one person's services or labour at the disposal of another, enabling the acquisition of that service itself. An independent contractor contract (contract for services or work) involves the independent contractor committing only to deliver the product or end result of their productive capacity, not the service itself.

title: When must a tax directive be obtained from SARS?
text: A tax directive must be obtained from SARS preferably where the employee is registered for Income Tax purposes in cases of retrenchment, retirement, or death.

title: What is the tax treatment when an employer rewards an employee for obtaining a qualification or successfully completing a study course?
text: Where an employer rewards an employee for obtaining a qualification or successful completion of a study course, such reward will represent taxable remuneration.

title: How are capital distributions that include further restricted equity instruments treated under the current rules?
text: Capital distributions that include further restricted equity instruments are treated as non-events, and the new restricted equity instrument is subject to section 8C. This treatment became effective in respect of any capital distribution or dividends received or accrued on or after 1 January 2011.

title: What percentage contribution rate must employers and employees pay to UIF on a monthly basis?
text: Both the employer and employee must contribute 1% of the remuneration paid or payable to the relevant employee during any month to UIF.

title: Under what circumstances can an estimate amount payable by an employer be subject to objection and appeal?
text: The estimate amount payable by employer is subject to objection and appeal unless both employer and Commissioner in terms of section 95(3) of Tax Administration Act agree in writing to said estimate assessment(s).

title: What source code and tax code should be used for commutation of a living annuity with effect from 1 March 2011?
text: Source code 3915 with tax code 4115 must be used on the certificate for commutation of a living annuity with effect from 1 March 2011.

title: What is the effective period for the increased donation deduction limit under COVID-19 Tax Relief?
text: The amendment allowing an additional limit of up to a maximum of 33.3% for three months or 16.66% for six months is effective from 1 April 2020 up until 30 September 2020.

title: What are the conditions for bursaries and scholarships to be exempt from tax for persons without disability?
text: Any bursary or bona fide bursary or scholarship other than any scholarship or bursary contemplated in paragraph (qA) granted to enable or assist any person to study at a recognised educational or research institution may be exempt in terms of Section 10(1)(q).

title: What is the effective date of this guide for employers?
text: The effective date of this guide is 12 March 2025.

title: What is the annual tax threshold for employees less than 65 years old in the 2026 tax year?
text: The annual tax threshold for employees less than 65 years old is R95 750.

title: What is the effective date of this guide for employers?
text: The effective date of this guide is 12 March 2025.

title: How should the medical scheme fees tax credit be apportioned when more than one person pays medical fees for the same person or dependent?
text: Where more than one person pays medical fees in respect of the benefits to a person or dependent, the amount allowable in respect of the medical scheme fees tax credit must be apportioned in the same ratio as the amount of the fees paid by that person bears to the total amount of fees payable.

title: What is the treatment of a lump sum paid by an employer to an employee upon normal termination of service?
text: The lump sum paid by an employer to an employee upon normal termination of service is treated as an annual payment (for example, service bonus) and the applicable formula is used for the calculation of employees' tax.

title: Under what condition does the exemption for scholarships or bursaries granted to an employee not apply based on remuneration proxy?
text: In the case of a scholarship or bursary granted to enable or assist any relative of an employee to study, if the remuneration proxy derived by the employee during the tax year exceeded R600,000.

title: What are the annual bursary limits for a family member with a disability pursuing higher education, when the employee's remuneration proxy exceeds R600,000?
text: The bursary limit is R90,000 in respect of higher education (NQF level 5 up to 10) for a family member with a disability whom the employee is liable for family care and support, provided the employee's remuneration proxy derived during the tax year exceeded R600,000.

title: Under what condition is an amount received by an employee under a share incentive scheme exempt from tax according to Section 10(1)(nE)?
text: An amount received by an employee under a share incentive scheme is exempt from tax if the employee does not receive or become entitled to receive any compensation or a consideration other than the repayment of any portion of the purchase price actually paid by him. This applies to cancellations of share purchase transactions or repurchases at a price not exceeding the original selling price.

title: How does unpaid leave affect an employee's tax period?
text: The fact that an employee has taken unpaid leave of any type (including maternity leave) has no effect on the employee's tax period. The tax period continues until the end of the tax year, unless the employee resigns or dies before the end of the tax year.

title: From what date do employer contributions to retirement funds become a taxable fringe benefit?
text: From 1 March 2016, contributions made or amounts paid by the employer on behalf of or for the benefit of the employee will be a taxable fringe benefit and a deemed contribution in the hands of the employee.

title: Which section of the guide covers information about employees 75 years or older?
text: Section 13.7 covers employees 75 years or older.

title: What are the three parties typically involved in a labour broker arrangement?
text: The three parties typically involved in a labour broker arrangement are the client, the labour broker, and the worker(s).

title: What remuneration is excluded for the purposes of SDL?
text: Remuneration excluded for SDL purposes includes: amounts paid to persons with a certificate of exemption issued under paragraph 2(5)(a) of the Fourth Schedule; amounts paid as pension, superannuation allowance or retiring allowance; amounts in terms of paragraphs (a), (d), (e) or (eA) of the definition of 'gross income' in section 1; and amounts payable to a learner in terms of a contract of employment as contemplated in section 18(3) of the SDL Act.

title: How should employers calculate the employees' tax deductible from gains on the disposal of qualifying equity shares?
text: Employers must calculate the employees' tax deductible from any amount received by or accrued to the employee during the year from any gain made from the disposal of any qualifying equity share or any right or interest in a qualifying equity share, in the same manner as tax on an annual payment (bonus).

title: For how long must employers keep their records?
text: Records must be kept for a period of five (5) years from the date of the submission of the return and from the end of the relevant tax period if the person is not required to submit a tax return but has earned some form of taxable income.

title: Why is a directive issued under Paragraph 2 (2B) of the Fourth Schedule for multiple sources of income?
text: A directive is issued under Paragraph 2 (2B) of the Fourth Schedule for multiple sources of income in order to alleviate the burden on persons receiving remuneration from more than one employer, particularly retirement income, who are often left with a tax debt due to under payment of PAYE.

title: Under which code should the tax portion be reflected if the lump sum payment reflects under code 3902, 3903, 3904, 3905, 3907, 3908, 3909 or 3614?
text: Code 4102

title: When can the Commissioner estimate the amount of Employees' tax, SDL or UIF due by an employer?
text: The Commissioner may estimate the amount based on information readily available when the employer has failed to furnish an EMP201 as required, or submitted a return or information that is incorrect or inadequate.

title: Which source code applies to withdrawals from a Pension or Provident fund with a date of accrual after 1 March 2009?
text: Code 3920 must be reflected on the certificate for withdrawals from a Pension, Pension preservation, Provident, Provident preservation or RAF with a date of accrual after 1 March 2009, except for paragraph (eA) of the definition of gross income in section 1.

title: Which source codes must be used for lump sum payments?
text: Either source code 3902 or 3904 must be used.

title: When did the Minister of Finance announce new tax rates and amendments for individuals?
text: The Minister of Finance announced new tax rates, tax rebates, tax thresholds and other tax amendments for individuals in his Budget Speech on 12 March 2025.

title: What code should be used to reflect restraint of trade payments on an IRP5/IT3(a) certificate?
text: Restraint of trade payments should be reflected under code 3613 on the certificate.

title: What is SDL and when did it come into operation?
text: SDL is a compulsory levy scheme for the purposes of funding education and training as envisaged in the Skills Development Act, 1998. This levy came into operation on 1 April 2000 and is payable by employers on a monthly basis.

title: What is the employer liable to pay to the Commissioner according to the context?
text: The employer shall be liable to the Commissioner for payment of the amount of employees' tax, SDL or UIF contributions estimated as if such an amount was deducted or withheld as required by the provisions of the relevant Tax Acts.

title: What payments constitute a taxable benefit and are subject to the deduction of employees' tax?
text: The following shall constitute a taxable benefit if payments are made by the employer and be subjected to the deduction of employees' tax: payments to reimburse the employee for loss on the sale of a previous residence during transfer; and/or architect's fees for the design or alteration of a new residence.

title: When is an annual equivalent calculation required?
text: An annual equivalent must be determined when an employee's tax period is shorter than a full tax year in order to determine the amount of Employees' Tax deductible.

title: Which public benefit organisations are exempt from SDL levy payment?
text: Public benefit organisations exempt from Income Tax in terms of section 10(1)(cN) that solely carry on welfare and humanitarian activities (paragraph 1 of Part 1 of the Ninth Schedule), health care (limited to paragraphs 2(a), (b), (c) and (d) of Part 1 of the Ninth Schedule), religion, belief or philosophy public benefit activities (paragraph 5 of Part 1 of the Ninth Schedule), or solely provide funds to such organisations (paragraph 10 of Part 1 of the Ninth Schedule), and to whom a letter of exemption has been issued by the SARS Tax Exemption Unit.

title: What are the offences related to IRP5/IT3(a) certificates?
text: Offences related to IRP5/IT3(a) certificates include: failing to deliver them to employees or former employees within prescribed periods; permitting a false IRP5/IT3(a) to be issued or knowingly being in possession of or using a false one; altering an IRP5/IT3(a) issued by another person; and issuing or causing to be issued any document purporting to be an IRP5/IT3(a) without being an employer or having authority from an employer.

title: Under what conditions may an employee earning commission apply for a tax directive?
text: An employee earning commission may only apply for a tax directive where his/her remuneration consists mainly in the form of commission based on the employee's sales or turnover attributable to him/her.

title: When must employers apply the percentage of employees' tax as indicated on the directive?
text: Employers must apply the percentage of employees' tax as indicated on the directive prior to taking into account allowable deductions for employees' tax purposes such as pension and retirement annuity fund contributions.

title: What is the effective date of the Guide for Employers in Respect of Employees' Tax for the 2026 tax year?
text: The effective date is 12 March 2025.

title: Does the exemption for foreign government employees cover all types of income?
text: No, the exemption does not apply to other income such as interest or rentals that the employee of a foreign government may earn while working in South Africa.

title: Are pensions received by South African residents from sources outside South Africa subject to normal tax?
text: No. Any pension received by or accrued to any resident from a source outside South Africa, which is not deemed to be from a source in South Africa, in consideration of past employment outside South Africa, is exempt from normal tax in South Africa.

title: When must a person register as a taxpayer if their only taxable income is a capital gain?
text: A person whose sole source of taxable income comprises a taxable capital gain needs to register as a taxpayer at a SARS branch office.

title: How are amounts distributed by collective investment schemes carried on outside South Africa treated in the hands of resident holders?
text: Amounts distributed by collective investment schemes to participatory holders are treated as foreign dividends in the hands of resident holders. These foreign dividends are subject to normal tax in South Africa, and resident holders are entitled to claim withholding tax imposed in respect of the foreign dividend as a credit against their South African normal tax liability.

title: What is the deadline for paying the withheld tax to SARS if the foreign entertainer or sportsperson is personally liable?
text: The foreign entertainer or sportsperson must pay the 15% tax to SARS within 30 days after the amount is received by or accrued to them.

title: What is the withholding tax rate for payments made to foreign entertainers and sportspersons in South Africa?
text: The withholding tax rate is 15% on all payments made to foreign entertainers and sportspersons.

title: Are employees in the domestic service of foreign government officials exempt from normal tax in South Africa, and if so, what conditions must they meet?
text: Yes, employees in the domestic service are exempt from normal tax in South Africa provided they are not South African citizens and are not ordinarily resident in South Africa.

title: What are the three main criteria in the physical presence test for determining residency status?
text: The physical presence test involves three criteria: (1) whether the person was physically present in the Republic for a period or periods exceeding 91 days in aggregate during the relevant tax year, (2) whether the person was physically present in the Republic for a period or periods exceeding 91 days in aggregate during each of the 5 years preceding the relevant tax year, and (3) whether the person was physically present in the Republic for a period or periods exceeding 915 days in aggregate during the preceding 5 tax years.

title: What are the three categories of waivers that can be granted by the Commissioner of SARS under subsection 9D(10)?
text: The three categories of waivers are: Business establishment waiver for related CFC group employees, equipment and facilities; Diversionary transaction waiver for centrally located operations; and Financial services comparably-taxed waiver.

title: What happens to the remuneration exemption if a foreign government employee applies for and receives a permit for permanent residence in South Africa?
text: The exemption falls away and liability for normal tax in South Africa arises from the date of issue of the permit for permanent residence.

title: What factor may affect the taxability of annuities received from abroad by South African residents?
text: The taxability of the annuity may be affected by an agreement for the avoidance of double taxation.

title: Under what conditions is income from a foreign trust taxable in the hands of a resident beneficiary?
text: Income from a foreign trust is taxable in the hands of a resident beneficiary where the resident has a vested right in the income, or where the trustees have exercised their discretion to distribute income (including capital that arose from amounts that would have constituted income) to a beneficiary who has not a vested right.

title: What is the email address for sending comments and suggestions regarding this brochure?
text: Comments and/or suggestions regarding this brochure may be sent to policycomments@sars.gov.za

title: What is the withholding tax rate on payments to foreign entertainers and sportspersons, and is it considered a final tax?
text: The withholding tax rate on foreign entertainers and sportspersons is 15%, and it is a final tax in respect of such payments received by or accrued to the foreign entertainer or sportsperson.

title: What is the tax obligation for a natural person who is considered a resident of South Africa?
text: A natural person who is considered a resident of South Africa is taxable on his or her worldwide income, that is, income derived from sources within and outside South Africa subject to certain exclusions.

title: On what income are non-residents taxed in South Africa?
text: A non-resident is only taxed on amounts received by or accrued to him or her from a source within South Africa.

title: Under what circumstances is the non-resident exemption not applicable in South Africa?
text: The non-resident exemption is not applicable if the non-resident was physically present in South Africa for a period exceeding 183 days in aggregate during the tax year, or at any time during the tax year carried on a business through a permanent establishment in South Africa.

title: What is the annual exclusion amount for capital gains and capital losses for a natural person in a normal tax year?
text: The first R16 000 of the sum of capital gains and capital losses in a tax year is excluded from capital gains tax for a natural person.

title: What was the date of the 4th issue of this brochure?
text: 31 July 2006

title: What are the day requirements for an employee to claim exemption on remuneration for services rendered outside South Africa?
text: An employee must be outside South Africa for a period or periods exceeding 183 full days in aggregate during any period of 12 months, and for a continuous period exceeding 60 full days during that period of 12 months, with services rendered during those periods.

title: What is the physical presence test and to whom does it apply?
text: The physical presence test is a time-based concept that is only applicable to a natural person who was not at any stage during the relevant tax year ordinarily resident in South Africa. It is based on the number of days during which a natural person is physically present in South Africa, where a day includes a part of a day, meaning both the day of arrival and departure are included in the count.

title: How can South African residents who receive foreign dividends avoid double taxation?
text: Resident holders of foreign dividends are entitled to claim withholding tax imposed in respect of the foreign dividend as a credit against their South African normal tax liability.

title: Under the physical presence test, when does a natural person who is not ordinarily resident in South Africa become a resident for income tax purposes?
text: A natural person who is not ordinarily resident in South Africa becomes a resident for income tax purposes as from the first day (beginning) of the sixth tax year if he or she is physically present in South Africa for the periods as set out in the requirements. The purpose of the presence is irrelevant, and a day is counted even if the presence is as a result of a holiday, visiting friends, or a funeral.

title: Which foreign currency assets are exempt from CGT?
text: Foreign currency notes and coins and traveller's cheques used for the regular payment of personal expenses (for example, during a holiday) are exempt from CGT. Additionally, a person is allowed one foreign bank account (a call or current account) free of CGT, provided that it is used for the regular (that is, monthly) payment of personal expenses.

title: What is the R3 200 exemption limit applied to in South Africa?
text: The R3 200 exemption limit is applied to foreign dividends and foreign interest received by or accrued to a resident, in that order of priority.

title: Under what conditions is the 15% withholding tax not applicable to a foreign entertainer or sportsperson?
text: The 15% withholding tax is not applicable when a foreign entertainer or sportsperson is employed by a South African employer and is physically present in South Africa for more than 183 days in aggregate in a 12-month period that begins or ends in a tax year. In these circumstances, they must pay normal tax on the same basis as a resident.

title: How are proceeds and expenditure translated into rand for foreign equity instruments?
text: For foreign equity instruments, proceeds from the sale of the asset are translated into rand at the average exchange rate for the tax year in which the asset was disposed of or at the spot rate on the date of disposal. Expenditure incurred in respect of that asset is translated into rand at the average exchange rate for the tax year during which it was incurred or the spot rate on the date on which it was incurred.

title: What are the two separate tests used to determine whether a natural person is a resident in South Africa?
text: The two separate tests are the ordinarily resident test and the physical presence test.

title: When was the 5th issue of this brochure published?
text: The 5th issue was published in March 2008

title: How can a non-resident's tax position be affected by agreements between South Africa and foreign countries?
text: A non-resident's tax position may be affected by an agreement for the avoidance of double taxation entered into between the Government of South Africa and the Government of the foreign country in which he or she resides.

title: In the example of Y, a Mexican resident seconded to South Africa for seven months, what income is subject to normal tax in South Africa?
text: The salary received by Y in South Africa during the seven-month period is subject to normal tax in South Africa as it is from a source in South Africa. Her worldwide income (except her salary earned in South Africa) is not subject to normal tax in South Africa.

title: Are dividends received by non-residents from South African resident companies subject to normal tax?
text: No, dividends received by or accrued to a non-resident from a source within South Africa (resident companies) are exempt from normal tax in South Africa. Dividends from collective investment schemes are also exempt from normal tax under the circumstances described in section 5.1.

title: When does a natural person who becomes ordinarily resident in South Africa start being subject to tax on worldwide income?
text: A natural person who becomes ordinarily resident in South Africa will be subject to tax on all worldwide income received by or accrued to them from the specific date they become ordinarily resident, excluding certain income that may be exempt.

title: Under what circumstances does a natural person who is a resident cease to be a resident?
text: A natural person who is a resident by virtue of the physical presence test ceases to be a resident if he or she is physically outside South Africa for a continuous period of at least 330 full days. The natural person ceases to be a resident as from the day immediately after the day on which he or she left South Africa for such a continuous period of at least 330 full days.

title: What happens to a person's resident status when they emigrate from South Africa?
text: A natural person who emigrates from South Africa to another country will cease to be a resident as from the date that he or she emigrates.

title: How is rental income taxed for non-residents in South Africa, and what deductions are available?
text: Rental income which arises in South Africa, received by or accrued to a non-resident will be subject to normal tax in South Africa. Expenses such as rates and taxes, bond interest, insurance and repairs may be claimed as deductions against such rental income, subject to certain conditions.

title: What contact options are provided for obtaining additional information about taxation?
text: Additional information concerning taxation can be obtained by: contacting your local SARS branch; visiting the SARS website at www.sars.gov.za; contacting your own tax advisors; calling the SARS National Call Centre on 0860 12 12 18 if calling locally; or calling +27 11 602 2093 if calling from abroad.

title: What is the annual exclusion amount for capital gains tax for a natural person in South Africa?
text: The annual exclusion is R16 000, which is increased to R120 000 where the person dies during the tax year.

title: What is the email address for sending comments and suggestions regarding this brochure?
text: policycomments@sars.gov.za

title: What happens to foreign taxes paid on income that is not subject to tax in South Africa?
text: Foreign taxes paid on income that is not subject to tax in South Africa are forfeited and cannot be carried forward.

title: What are some events that trigger a disposal for capital gains tax purposes?
text: Events that trigger a disposal include a sale, donation, exchange, loss, death and cessation of residence in South Africa.

title: How is the capital element of a purchased annuity treated for tax purposes in South Africa?
text: The capital element of a purchased annuity is exempt from normal tax. The certificate issued by the insurance company will reflect the capital content.

title: Do individuals need to register separately for CGT if they are already registered as taxpayers?
text: No, a person need not register separately for CGT if he or she is already registered as a taxpayer for normal tax purposes, as CGT forms part of the income tax system.

title: What is the minimum number of days a person must be physically present in South Africa during the current tax year to meet the physical presence test requirement?
text: A natural person must be physically present in South Africa for a period or periods exceeding 91 days in aggregate during the tax year under consideration.

title: According to the ordinarily resident test, what does it mean for a natural person to be ordinarily resident in South Africa?
text: A natural person is ordinarily resident in South Africa if his or her permanent home, to which he or she will normally return, is in South Africa. A continuous physical presence is not a prerequisite to be ordinarily resident in South Africa. The concept refers to living in a place with some degree of continuity, apart from accidental or temporary absence.

title: Can a resident claim both a rebate for foreign taxes paid and relief under a double taxation agreement?
text: No. The rebate for foreign taxes paid may be granted in substitution for the relief to which a resident would be entitled under a double taxation agreement and is not granted in addition to such relief.

title: What are the two exchange rate options available for converting foreign currency gains on immovable property to rand?
text: The two options are: the average exchange rate for the tax year in which the asset was disposed of, or the spot rate on the date of disposal of the asset.

title: What is the increased annual exclusion amount during the tax year in which a natural person dies?
text: The annual exclusion increases to R120 000 in the tax year during which a natural person dies.

title: How is taxable income derived from gross income?
text: Taxable income is derived by deducting the aggregate amount of certain exemptions from gross income to arrive at the amount of income, then deducting the aggregate amount of allowable deductions from the amount of income, and adding the amount of all taxable capital gains to the amount of income.

title: Is a non-resident's salary earned in South Africa subject to normal tax?
text: Yes, salary income earned in South Africa by a non-resident will be subject to normal tax in South Africa, unless an agreement for the avoidance of double taxation between South Africa and the foreign country stipulates otherwise.

title: What are the categories of diversionary income of a CFC that section 9D targets?
text: Section 9D targets diversionary income of a CFC which is derived from the sale by a CFC of certain goods to a connected person who is a resident, the sale by a CFC of certain goods where the CFC purchased those goods or intermediary inputs from connected persons who are residents, the performance of certain services by a CFC to a connected person who is a resident, and the granting by that CFC of the use, right of use or permission to use an intangible asset to a connected person who is a resident.

title: Do people already registered as taxpayers need to register separately for CGT?
text: No, a person need not register separately for CGT if he or she is already registered as a taxpayer for normal tax purposes.

title: Under what conditions is a non-resident employed by South African government entities exempt from normal tax?
text: A non-resident employed in the national or provincial spheres of government, any municipality, or any national or provincial public entity is exempt from normal tax if 80% or more of the expenses are defrayed from funds voted by Parliament to render services outside South Africa, the remuneration is taxed in the employee's country of residence, and the foreign tax is not paid on their behalf by the employing entities.

title: What is the definition of 'net income' of a CFC according to section 9D(2A)?
text: The net income of a CFC in respect of a foreign tax year is defined in section 9D(2A) as an amount equal to the taxable income of the CFC determined in accordance with the provisions of the Act as if the CFC had been a taxpayer, and as if it had been a resident for purposes of the definition of 'gross income', and certain other sections and paragraphs of the Eighth Schedule to the Act.

title: How are annuities from trusts or insurance companies received by residents from abroad treated for tax purposes?
text: Annuities (other than pensions or annuities referred to above) such as from a trust, insurance company etc received by residents from abroad, that is, from a source outside South Africa, are subject to normal tax in South Africa.

title: Is foreign interest received by a resident subject to normal tax in South Africa?
text: Yes, foreign interest received by or accrued to a resident is subject to normal tax in South Africa.

title: What expenses can be deducted from foreign rental income in South Africa?
text: Expenses such as bond interest, rates and taxes, insurance and repairs may be claimed as a deduction, subject to certain conditions.

title: What is the tax treatment of foreign taxes paid on income that is not subject to tax in South Africa?
text: Foreign taxes paid on income that is not subject to tax in South Africa are forfeited and cannot be carried forward.

title: Is this guide a binding legal document?
text: No, this guide is not meant to delve into the precise technical and legal detail associated with taxation and should not be used as a legal reference. It is not a binding general ruling issued in accordance with section 76P of the Income Tax Act, No. 58 of 1962.

title: What is a permanent establishment in the context of foreign business income?
text: A permanent establishment is a fixed place of business through which the business of the enterprise is wholly or partly carried on. It is defined in double taxation agreements and determines whether the source country may tax profits in addition to the country of residence.

title: Under what conditions is a portion of a pension payable to a non-resident taxable in South Africa?
text: A portion of a pension payable to a non-resident is taxable in South Africa if services were rendered inside and outside of South Africa and at least two years out of the last 10 years of services (before the accrual of the pension) were rendered in South Africa. The taxable portion is calculated in the ratio which the number of years' service rendered in South Africa bears to the total period during which services were rendered.

title: What is the taxable amount of foreign dividends if the total foreign dividends are R2,500 with an exemption of R2,400?
text: The taxable amount of foreign dividends is R100, calculated as R2,500 less R2,400 exemption.

title: Which assets must non-residents account for capital gains and capital losses on?
text: Non-residents must account for capital gains and capital losses made from the disposal of immovable property situated in South Africa or any interest or right in immovable property situated in South Africa, and assets attributable to a permanent establishment in South Africa.

title: What types of passive, investment income does section 9D target?
text: Section 9D targets passive, investment type income which comprises dividends, interest, royalties, rental income, annuities, insurance premiums, and capital gains on assets from which the above income is or could be earned.

title: How is double taxation typically resolved for residents deriving foreign business income?
text: Double taxation is normally resolved through the application of an agreement for the avoidance of double taxation concluded between the two countries. Normally, profits will only be taxed in the country of residence unless the business is carried on through a permanent establishment in the other country.

title: How are foreign tax credits divided between spouses married in community of property?
text: Foreign tax credits relating to income deemed to have accrued in equal shares to both spouses must be divided equally between the two spouses.

title: Under what circumstances is the exemption on interest income not applicable to non-residents?
text: The exemption on interest received by or accrued to a non-resident from a source within South Africa is not applicable if the non-resident was physically present in South Africa for a period exceeding 183 days in aggregate during the tax year, or at any time during the tax year carried on a business through a permanent establishment in South Africa.

title: Which government employees are not eligible for exemption from normal tax on foreign employment income?
text: Residents who are employed in the national or provincial spheres of government, any local authority or any public entity if 80% or more of the expenses of these entities are defrayed from funds voted by Parliament are not eligible for the exemption.

title: How are foreign tax credits divided between spouses married in community of property?
text: Foreign tax credits relating to income deemed to have accrued in equal shares to both spouses must be divided equally between the two spouses.

title: Under what circumstances are royalties and 'know-how' payments deemed to be derived from a source within South Africa?
text: Royalties and 'know-how' payments are deemed to be derived from a source within South Africa if received by or accrued to a person by virtue of the use, right of use or grant of permission to use in South Africa of any patent, design, trademark, copyright, model, pattern, plan, formula or process or similar property, or motion picture film/video/sound recording/advertising matter, or the imparting of or undertaking to impart any scientific, technical, industrial or commercial knowledge or information for use in South Africa.

title: What is an example of when a non-resident might need to register as a taxpayer in South Africa?
text: A non-resident who has no other South African-source income and disposes of immovable property in South Africa may need to register as a taxpayer.

title: Do special rules apply to immovable property bought in one foreign currency and disposed of in another?
text: Yes, special rules apply to immovable property bought in one foreign currency and disposed of in another, as well as to assets attributable to a foreign permanent establishment where financial reporting is in another foreign currency.

title: What method does South Africa use to avoid double taxation?
text: South Africa uses the credit method. Under this method, a resident who is taxable in South Africa on income received from a foreign country and who is liable to tax in the foreign country on that income will be allowed a credit for the foreign tax paid against his or her normal tax liability in South Africa.

title: Are days spent in transit through South Africa between two places outside South Africa counted toward the physical presence test?
text: No. Any day that a person is in transit through South Africa between two places outside South Africa and that person does not formally enter South Africa through a port of entry, or at any other place as may be permitted by the Director-General of the Department of Home Affairs or the Minister of Home Affairs, is excluded in the count.

title: Is this guide considered an official publication under the Tax Administration Act?
text: No, this guide is not an 'official publication' as defined in section 1 of the Tax Administration Act 28 of 2011 and accordingly does not create a practice generally prevailing under section 5 of that Act.

title: What is the capital gain threshold for residents who must submit an income tax return?
text: A resident must submit an income tax return if they had a capital gain or capital loss exceeding R40 000.

title: What is the basic amount for provisional tax estimates and when can it be lower?
text: The basic amount is the individual's taxable income as assessed by SARS for the previous year of assessment. The estimate may not be lower than this basic amount unless permission is obtained from SARS.

title: What are the contact hours for the SARS Service Centre on Wednesdays?
text: On Wednesdays, the SARS Service Centre can be called between 9am and 4.30pm South African time.

title: What information does an IRP5 certificate disclose?
text: An IRP5 discloses, amongst other things, the total employment income earned for the year of assessment and the employees' tax that was deducted or withheld by the employer and paid over to SARS.

title: What is the primary purpose of the Guide on Income Tax and the Individual?
text: The purpose of this guide is to inform individuals who are South African residents of their income tax commitments under the Income Tax Act 58 of 1962.

title: What are the two broad categories of penalties introduced by the TA Act on 1 October 2012?
text: The two broad categories of penalties introduced by the TA Act on 1 October 2012 are administrative non-compliance penalties in terms of Chapter 15 of the TA Act, intended mainly to promote compliance with the administrative provisions of a tax Act, and understatement penalties in terms of Chapter 16 of the TA Act, intended to deter conduct that flouts the law.

title: What is the deadline for an individual to submit an original return if they disagree with an auto-assessment for the 2025 year of assessment?
text: An individual can submit the original return by either the due date for submission of tax returns by all taxpayers who have been auto-assessed (20 October 2025), or 40 business days from the date of the assessment, whichever is longer.

title: What are the two categories of lump sum benefits from retirement funds?
text: The two categories of lump sum benefits are retirement fund lump sum benefits and retirement fund lump sum withdrawal benefits.

title: What is the once-off threshold amount for retirement fund lump sum benefits before they become taxable?
text: The once-off threshold amount is R550,000 for retirement fund lump sum benefits, which is only available to an individual once-off.

title: How can the income tax payable by individuals be reduced according to the Minister of Finance?
text: The income tax payable by individuals can be reduced if everyone pays his or her fair share of income tax – the more of us who pay, the less we all have to pay.

title: What is the deadline for lodging an appeal after SARS disallows or partially allows an objection?
text: An individual must lodge the appeal within 30 business days after SARS delivered the notice of disallowance or partial allowance of the objection. This period may be extended by 21 business days if a senior SARS official is satisfied that reasonable grounds exist for the delay, or up to 45 business days if a senior SARS official is satisfied that exceptional circumstances exist for the delay.

title: What are examples of criminal offences relating to filing a return without authority?
text: Criminal offences relating to filing a return without authority occur where a person submits a return or other document to SARS under a forged signature, uses another person's electronic or digital signature in an electronic communication to SARS without that other person's consent and authority, or submits to SARS a communication on behalf of another person without that other person's consent and authority.

title: What penalty rate applies to late or non-payment of provisional tax?
text: An individual will be liable for a penalty of 10% on the late or non-payment of provisional tax.

title: What are the allowed methods to submit an appeal in the automated dispute management workflow?
text: The appeal can only be submitted via eFiling guided process or at a SARS service centre by appointment only.

title: What happens to tax payable on previous lump sums when calculating tax on the current lump sum?
text: Tax payable on previous lump sums is deducted from the total tax payable to arrive at the tax payable on the current lump sum

title: Under what circumstances is a retirement fund lump sum benefit payable?
text: A retirement fund lump sum benefit is payable upon retirement, death, or termination of employment due to redundancy or an employer ceasing trade.

title: Can income tax rebates result in a refund or credit situation?
text: No, rebates cannot result in a refund or credit situation. They are limited to the income tax due and can only reduce the income tax due, not create a refund or credit.

title: What is the only non-compliance currently subject to a fixed amount penalty?
text: Currently the only non-compliance that could be subject to a fixed amount penalty is failure by a person to submit an income tax return by the due date.

title: What are the two main methods used by SARS to collect income tax?
text: The two main methods used by SARS to collect income tax are: (1) the withholding of employees' tax by an employer on remuneration paid to an employee, which is subsequently paid to SARS on a monthly basis, and (2) the payment of provisional tax by selected individuals, which is collected by SARS on a six-monthly basis.

title: What is the maximum percentage for understatement penalties?
text: The understatement penalty could be up to 200% of the tax shortfall.

title: What are the interest thresholds that allow an individual to avoid submitting an income tax return based on local interest income?
text: Local interest (other than from a tax-free investment) not exceeding R23 800 if the individual is below the age of 65, or R34 500 if the individual is 65 years or older allows an individual to not submit an income tax return.

title: What are the maximum penalties for criminal offences related to non-compliance with tax obligations?
text: If convicted of criminal offences relating to non-compliance, a person is subject to a fine or to imprisonment for a period not exceeding two years.

title: How does SARS customize the income tax return for an individual?
text: SARS customizes the income tax return according to the complexity of the individual's tax affairs, ensuring that the income tax return only contains the relevant fields for completion to suit the individual's specific needs.

title: What is the maximum period for an extension granted by SARS?
text: The period of any extension granted by SARS may not exceed a period of 40 business days, or the date that the original assessment prescribes, whichever is longer.
